---
title: "Multilingual webpages with babelquarto"
description:
  Do you speak my language?
date: 2024-12-06
date-modified: today
image: https://i.giphy.com/W69vZGazsH2LDnKCzY.webp
citation:
  url: 2024-12-06_babelquarto
lang: en
categories:
  - R
  - blogging
  - Quarto
---

## Say hi to `babelquarto`

[Quarto](https://quarto.org) is an amazingly flexible tool connecting the data analysis powers of languages like R, python, and Julia to output formats ranging from PDF to websites. New features are rapidly released, but one that has been lacking so far is support for multilingual websites[^1]. Fortunately, developers (namely `@maelle`) at [rOpenSci](https://ropensci.org/) have stepped up to fill the gap with the `babelquarto` R package. Here, I demonstrate how to use it and point out some pitfalls to be aware of.

[^1]: The `lang` option [can be used](https://quarto.org/docs/authoring/language.html) to change the *overall* language of a website, but this does not allow selecting from among multiple languages.

## Getting started

Here, I assume you want to add multilingual support to an existing webpage. For demonstration purposes, let's use the default one generated by `quarto create project` (or `quarto_create_project()` if using the `quarto` R package):

```{r}
#| label: create-site-hide
#| echo: false
library(quarto)
library(babelquarto)
library(fs)

# Create the website in a temporary directory.
temp_dir <- tempdir()

project_path <- fs::path(temp_dir, "example")

if (fs::dir_exists(project_path)) {
  fs::dir_delete(project_path)
}

quarto_create_project(
  name = "example",
  type = "website",
  dir = temp_dir,
  quiet = TRUE,
  no_prompt = TRUE
)
```

```{r}
#| label: create-site-show
#| eval: false
library(quarto)
library(babelquarto)
library(fs)

# Create the website in a temporary directory.
temp_dir <- tempdir()

quarto_create_project(
  name = "example",
  type = "website",
  dir = temp_dir
)
```

Let's check the contents of the new website:

```{r}
#| label: show-website-1
dir_tree(path(temp_dir, "example"))
```

And inspect `_quarto.yml` in particular:

```{r}
#| label: show-quarto-yml-1
readr::read_lines(path(temp_dir, "example", "_quarto.yml")) |>
  cat(sep = "\n")
```

## Register the main language

The next step is to modify `_quarto.yml` by registering the main language of the website (in other words, the default language that appears when accessing the main URL). Here, we will register Japanese as the main language:

```{r}
#| label: register-main-lang
register_main_language(
  main_language = "ja",
  project_path = path(temp_dir, "example")
)
```

This will modify `_quarto.yml`:

```{r}
#| label: show-quarto-yml-2
readr::read_lines(path(temp_dir, "example", "_quarto.yml")) |>
  cat(sep = "\n")
```
